claim.hypotheses_location: Section 1.4 Research questions (Hypotheses 1a, 1b, and Hypothesis 2), page 78

data.source: Secondary analysis of the ELEMENT study (Assessment of Reading and Mathematics Development), a representative school-based assessment from Berlin with tests and questionnaires
data.wave_or_subset: Sixth-grade elementary school sample (outcomes); general cognitive abilities measured in 4th grade
data.sample_size: 2835 students nested in 134 elementary school classes
data.notes: Bilingual group identification based on parent-reported (and if missing, student-reported) regular home language use; missing data handled via multiple imputation (5 imputed datasets) using a specified background model; clustering by classes accounted for in analyses (type=complex); English and German tests scaled using one-parameter IRT in ConQuest with WLE person estimates (English WLE scaled M=100, SD=20); parent questionnaire response rate ~60%; attrition between 4th and 6th grade ~7%.

method.description: Secondary analyses compared English foreign language achievement between monolingual and immigrant bilingual students, accounting for background characteristics and instructional language proficiency. English achievement was measured with a Cloze test scaled via IRT. Bilingual status and specific home-language groups were derived from questionnaires. Multiple regression models, with clustering and multiple imputation, assessed associations and group differences, including the role of German reading proficiency.
steps: Identify monolingual and bilingual students from parent reports of regular home language use (use student report if parent data missing); form specific bilingual groups (Arabic-German, Chinese-German, Polish-German, Turkish-German, and Other).
Compute English language achievement from a Cloze test (four texts, 91 items), scaled using one-parameter IRT in ConQuest; use WLE person parameters (M=100, SD=20).
Measure German reading proficiency (32 items across four texts; one-parameter IRT; WLEs) and form five proficiency-based bilingual subgroups (Levels 1–5).

Assemble control variables: general cognitive abilities (CFT 4-12R verbal and figural analogies composite, measured in 4th grade), age, gender, socio-economic status (HISEI), parental education (5-point scale), and cultural capital (books at home, 4-point scale).
Handle missing data via multiple imputation (five datasets) using a background model including individual- and classroom-level factors.

Estimate multiple linear regression models: (a) bilingual vs monolingual; (b) bilingual German proficiency levels vs monolingual; (c) specific bilingual language groups vs monolingual; sequentially add controls, then add German reading proficiency. Account for class-level clustering (type=complex) and combine imputation results.
Report coefficients, significance, and explained variance (R^2) across models; compare patterns across language groups.
method.models: Multiple linear regression with dummy variables for group comparisons; cluster-robust inference (type=complex) with multiple imputation. Test scaling via one-parameter IRT (Rasch) to obtain WLEs.
method.outcome_variable: English language achievement (Cloze test WLE, scaled M=100, SD=20)
method.independent_variables: Bilingual status (bilingual vs monolingual), bilingual German reading proficiency level (five dummy variables, Levels 1–5), bilingual language group dummies (Arabic-German, Chinese-German, Polish-German, Turkish-German, Other; reference: monolingual), and in extended models German reading proficiency (standardized).
method.control_variables: General cognitive abilities (CFT 4-12R composite), age, gender, socio-economic status (HISEI), parental education, cultural capital (books at home).
method.tools_software: Mplus 5.21 (type=complex; type=imputation); ACER ConQuest for IRT scaling

results.summary: Uncontrolled comparisons showed bilingual students scored lower in English than monolinguals. After controlling for cognitive abilities, age, gender, socio-economic status, parental education, and cultural capital, bilingual status was positively associated with English achievement, though the effect was small. Higher German (instructional language) reading proficiency among bilinguals corresponded to higher English outcomes; when German proficiency and background factors were controlled, most bilingual language groups (Chinese-German, Polish-German, and Other bilinguals) showed significant advantages over monolinguals, and Turkish-German students showed a significant advantage once German proficiency was included. Arabic-German students did not differ significantly from monolinguals after controls. German reading proficiency was a strong predictor of English achievement, and models including it explained substantially more variance (R^2 up to 0.46).

numerical_results.outcome_name: Effect of German reading proficiency on English (standardized predictor; Model D)
Chinese-German vs monolingual (Model C, with background controls)
Polish-German vs monolingual (Model C, with background controls)
Other bilingual vs monolingual (Model C, with background controls)
Turkish-German vs monolingual (Model D, with background and German proficiency controls)
Arabic-German vs monolingual (Model C, with background controls)

metadata.original_paper_title: The effect of speaking a minority language at home on foreign language learning
